manifest.name: Studio Docs
manifest.description: Studio host (studio.*) tool catalog + concepts. Knowledge-only bundle — no UI, no agents. Auto-loaded as seed at host boot, knowledge.sources fanned out as studio://knowledge/<src>/<doc> MCP resources.

agents.name: Studio Manager
agents.systemPrompt:
You are the AppPlayer Studio manager. Your sole job is to coordinate host inventory and delegate to specialist agents.

The "Available tools" section below your prompt lists the EXACT tools you have access to in this session. They are real and callable — invoke them directly to answer the user. Do not greet the user, do not echo the routing rules, do not ask for clarification on straightforward queries. Do the work.

Built-in domains:
  • app_builder   — author mcp_ui_dsl bundles (`app_builder.*` / `studio.builder.*`).
  • scene_builder — record demo / tutorial scenarios (`studio.recorder.*` / `studio.scenario.*`).
  • makemind_ops  — workspace operations (`makemind_ops.*`).

Routing:
  • Agent listing / id query → call `bk.agent.list` immediately and return the result.
  • Agent metadata / single record → call `bk.agent.get`.
  • Dispatch to a specialist agent → call `bk.agent.dispatch` with the target agent id.
  • Bundle listing / install query → call the matching `host.*` / `<bundle>.*` tool from the catalog.
  • Knowledge / fact query → call `bk.knowledge.query` / `bk.fact.query`.
  • Ambiguous intent (only when truly ambiguous) → ask one short clarification question.

Never guess identifiers — call the relevant `*.list` tool first when you need them. Answer in the user's language.

sources.title: Studio host tools (studio.*) catalog
sources.description: Always-registered studio.* MCP tools — host-side surface every domain shares. Source = docs/06_OPS/reference/studio-tool-catalog.md.

documents.title: Overview — host vs domain surface
documents.content: Studio host registers studio.* tools at boot. They are available regardless of which domain is active. Domains (App Builder · Scene Builder · …) layer their own tools on top via BuiltInApp.registerHostTools. The mcp connection at system URL (default :7831) multiplexes all domains. Per-domain narrow links (inheritFromSystem=false) get studio.* mirror + single domain only (see MOD-INFRA-010).

documents.title: host capability packs — io · channel · browser · fs · db · canvas · kv · analysis · form · ingest
documents.content:
Beyond `studio.*`, the host registers reusable **capability tool packs** (`<id>.*`) — shared by every domain and bundle app, not per-domain. Logic lives in the `mcp_*` packages / the `capability_tools` recipe; the host only wires adapters + policy (built-in = UI + wiring). Design: `docs/03_DDD/capability-tools.md`.

- **io.\*** (sandboxed OS exec — exe allowlist git/dart/flutter/pub · allowedRoots jail · manager/operator roles): `io.execute` (policy-gated) · `io.plan_execute`→`io.commit_execute` (dangerous → dry-run → commit) · `io.read` · `io.subscribe` · `io.list_devices`/`describe_device`/`connect_device`/`disconnect_device` · `io.list_jobs`/`get_job`/`cancel_job`/`emergency_stop`. Process steps run dev commands (git/dart/flutter/pub) through `io.execute`.
- **channel.\*** (bidirectional messaging — in-app feed + connectors): `channel.send` · `channel.receive` · `channel.list` · `channel.status` · `channel.session.history`. Handoffs / notify route here.
- **browser.\*** (shared headless browser): `page_view` · `page_audit_role` · `web_search` · `extract` · `crawl` · `monitor` · `submit_form` · `download` · `open_login` · `auth_capture` · `page_compare_actors`.
- **fs.\*** (datastore filesystem — jailed to the config root): `read` · `write` · `edit` · `list` · `glob` · `stat` · `mkdir` · `move` · `remove` (destructive → needs an explicit commit).
- **db.\*** (sqlite datastore): `query` · `exec` · `tx`.
- **canvas.\*** (CDL 2D/3D engine): `cdl_to_json` · `json_to_cdl` · `validate_cdl`.
- **kv.\*** (key/value store): `set` · `get` · `exists` · `keys` · `remove`.
- **analysis.\*** (spec-driven analysis): `run` · `list_specs` · `get_job`.
- **form.\*** (template/binding + 5 renderers): `create_document` · `render` · `patch` · `validate` · `export` · `list_templates` · `get_status`.
- **ingest.\*** (document chunking → facts): `ingest.run`.

All host-shared: every workspace/agent reaches them via `<id>.*` (no per-domain registration). Datastore writes are role-gated (manager/operator); destructive ops (`fs.remove`) require commit. NOTE: `fs.*` here is the **datastore** capability, distinct from `studio.fs.*` (workspace file tools).

Usage patterns (per capability) = `studio://knowledge/studio_capability_recipes/*`.

documents.title: studio.agent.* — agent runtime
documents.content: studio.agent.list / describe / dispatch / history / clear_history / set_model. Manages registered LLM agents (manifest.agents[] entries from active bundles). dispatch sends one turn to a specific agent and returns text + tool_use blocks. set_model swaps model id at runtime.

documents.title: studio.builder.* — bundle mutator (Studio domain)
documents.content: Atomic mutators for the active Studio domain bundle (NOT App bundles). readManifest / readBundle / writeUI / patchManifest / addAgent / addTool / addSkill / addProfile / addPhilosophy / addSlashCommand / addDomainAction / addKnowledge* / addSettings* / writeScenario / ui.{addNode, setProp, moveNode, removeNode, reorderChildren, applyPatch, findNodes, readNode, readTree, diff, catalog.list, catalog.schema, catalog.diag} / lib.{create, delete, list, placeAsTemplate, placeInline, read, rename, render}. App bundles use App Builder's vibe_* tools (or app_builder.<family>.<verb> aliases) instead — see app_builder seed docs.

documents.title: studio.bundle.* — package lifecycle + resources
documents.content: install / uninstall / activate / list / list_files / read_file / list_assets / read_asset / dispatch_tool. install accepts .mcpb archive or .mbd/ dir, registers in bundle pool. activate makes a tab. list returns installed bundles. read_file/read_asset for arbitrary file access within bundle root. dispatch_tool invokes a bundle-declared tool.

documents.title: studio.chrome.* — shell wiring
documents.content: list_tabs / select_tab / close_tab / reload_tab / create_package / open_{settings, history, agents, onboarding, seed} / peek_tab_bar / set_tab_bar / toggle_left_panel / set_left_panel. Drives tab strip, left panel, header dialogs. select_tab takes key (path or 'home').

documents.title: studio.project.* — active bundle's project lifecycle
documents.content: info / close / new / open / recents / create / export. Operates on the CURRENT TAB's project context — App Builder's currentProject, Scene Builder's scene project, etc. project.new scaffolds via the active bundle's newProjectInActive bridge slot. project.info returns {packageName, packagePath, projectPath, projectName} or {} on home tab.

documents.title: studio.fs.* — workspace-gated file IO
documents.content: list / read / write / mkdir / delete / glob. Bounded to workspaceDir — paths outside throw. Use for project-bound file access. App bundle authoring should prefer vibe_file_* via active App Builder (project-root scoped) — studio.fs.* is broader workspace scope. `glob({pattern, path?, limit?})` walks recursive matching `**`/`*` (e.g. `**/manifest.json`, `ui/**/*.json`); returns `{count, matches:[absPath], truncated?}` capped at limit (default 500).

documents.title: studio.knowledge.* — RAG over registered knowledge sources
documents.content: query / test / validate. query({text, limit?}) returns top-N hits across all registered sources (seed bundles' knowledge.sources fanned out as resources). test validates query interpretation. validate runs lint on the active bundle's knowledge entries.

documents.title: studio.ui.* — UI control
documents.content: Full pointer + keyboard + focus + dropdown automation via Flutter binding (no macOS accessibility). · `studio.ui.set_center_mode` (UI/Bundle/Debug 3-way switch). · `studio.ui.tap` ({elementId or x+y, mode?:tap|long|double}) — synthetic click; elementId resolves via layout-snapshot rect resolver (accepts bare `id` OR `<type>:<key>`). · `studio.ui.type` ({text, elementId?, clear?, submit?}) — focused TextField TextEditingController direct set (bypasses IME / Korean+CJK garble); submit:true fires onSubmitted (Enter). · `studio.ui.dismiss_dialog` — pop topmost route on root Navigator. · `studio.ui.scroll` ({elementId or x+y, dx, dy}) — wheel scroll. · `studio.ui.hover` — pointer hover only (no click) for tooltip/hover decoration capture. · `studio.ui.right_click` — secondary-button tap (onSecondaryTap / context menu). · `studio.ui.drag` ({from, to, steps?}) — splitter / drag-and-drop / canvas pan. · `studio.ui.focus` — first EditableText.focusNode.requestFocus() without tap. · `studio.ui.element_value` — read EditableText `{value, hasFocus, selection}` (inverse of `type`). · `studio.ui.key` ({key:enter|escape|tab|arrow*|backspace|space, char?, modifiers:[shift|ctrl|alt|meta]}) — macOS keyevent platform channel. · `studio.ui.popover_entries` — enumerate active PopupMenu/Dropdown items as `[{value, label, enabled, rect}]`.

documents.title: studio.debug.* — diagnostic surface (~24 tools)
documents.content: studio.debug.* — 28 tools. boot/notify/dispatch_log · activation/bundles/tabs/chrome/runtimes/runtime_state/agents/chat/header_actions · config/settings/overrides · screenshot/layout_snapshot/snapshot_diff · servers (DomainServerManager pool) · history.{list,diff,restore} · dispatch_tool · set_state · wait_for · workspace_snapshot. NEW (2026-05-20): debug.overrides ({path?}) reads package_settings/*.json per-domain inherit/url overrides · debug.workspace_snapshot lists workspaceDir mbds+projects+other entries.

documents.title: studio.renderer.* — domain UI activation
documents.content: activate / current_view / layout_snapshot / screenshot / screenshot_region / image_diff. Drives the active domain UI mount. `layout_snapshot` walks chrome captureRoot AND adds `WidgetsBinding.rootElement` walk so showDialog overlays surface too. `image_diff` ({a, b base64 PNGs, threshold?}) returns `{score, diffPixels, totalPixels, w, h}` for visual regression — both inputs must share dimensions.

documents.title: studio.overlay.* — in-frame markup
documents.content: push / list / remove / clear. Annotation/highlight markers on top of the renderer. Used by Scene Builder for scenario overlays.

documents.title: studio.recorder.* — frame capture + encoding
documents.content: start / stop / status / recordings.list / encode / mark / caption. Captures frames at fps, encodes to mp4. `status` returns `markers[]` + `captions[]` (timeline `{label or text, startMs/elapsedMs, durationMs}`). `mark({label})` drops a chapter marker, `caption({text, durationMs?})` logs a subtitle (pair with `studio.overlay.push` for burn-in). Used by Scene Builder for scenario recordings + tutorial videos.

documents.title: studio.scenario.* — scenario list/run/save
documents.content: list / read / preview / run / save. Scene Builder's scenario format. preview = dry-run, run = with side effects.

documents.title: studio.scene.project.* — Scene Builder project lifecycle
documents.content: info / new / open. Sibling to studio.project.* but scene-specific (different storage layout).

documents.title: studio.chat.send — chat composer programmatic input
documents.content: Send a chat turn through the active tab's composer. Same path as user typing in chat panel.

documents.title: studio.{settings, search, workspace, meta}
documents.content: settings.get({key, path?}) - read system or domain override (per-package). settings.set({key, value, path?}) - write system OR per-domain override; **domain set triggers DomainServerManager.detach+attach automatically (runtime re-attach, no restart needed)**. System settings include `themeMode` (system/light/dark) and `debugMode` (boolean). When `debugMode=true`, every DslWorkspaceView mounts its bundle through `MCPUIRuntime.withInspector(widgetWrapper:)` so every rendered widget is tagged with MetaData — required for `studio.renderer.layout_snapshot` to surface inner-bundle widgets and for `studio.ui.tap({elementId:...})` to address them by name. Off by default (production fast path); flip true for scripted UI tests / tutorial recordings / external-LLM debugging. Toggle is live (no restart) — DslWorkspaceView listens on `ChromeBridge.inspectorEnabled` and re-mounts when it flips. search.query - workspace-wide text search. workspace.adopt - bring external folder into workspace mode. meta.list_tools / describe_tool - runtime tool discovery.

sources.title: Host capability recipes — io · datastore · canvas · kv · analysis · channel · ingest · form
sources.description: Usage patterns (not just the verb list) for the shared host capability packs. Pairs with the studio_host_tools `capabilities` catalog. Agents drive real work — commands, files, db, rendering, messaging — through these.

documents.title: io.* — sandboxed OS execution
documents.content:
# io.* — sandboxed OS execution

Run dev commands only (`git` · `dart` · `flutter` · `pub`) under policy: exe allowlist, `allowedRoots` jail (config root), manager/operator roles, no shell.

| do | call |
|---|---|
| safe command | `io.execute({exe:'git', argv:['status']})` → `{exitCode, stdout, stderr}` |
| dangerous (push/publish) | `io.plan_execute` (dry-run + risk) → `io.commit_execute` (after sign-off) |
| long job | `io.get_job` · `io.cancel_job` · `io.emergency_stop` |

Read-only subcommands (analyze/test/version/status/log/diff/get) run unattended; mutating ones gate on the process's approval step. Pattern — a process step does real work:
```
step: { skillId: io.execute, inputs: { exe: dart, argv: [analyze] } }
```

documents.title: fs.* / db.* — datastore (filesystem + sqlite)
documents.content:
# fs.* / db.* — datastore (filesystem + sqlite)

`fs.*` is jailed to the config root (a path escaping it is rejected). `db.*` is a sqlite source. Distinct from `studio.fs.*` (workspace files).

| do | call |
|---|---|
| read / write a file | `fs.read({path})` · `fs.write({path, text})` |
| edit in place | `fs.edit({path, oldString, newString})` |
| list / find | `fs.list({path})` · `fs.glob({pattern})` |
| delete (destructive) | `fs.remove({path})` → `needs_commit`; requires explicit plan→commit |
| read query | `db.query({statement, params})` → `{rows}` |
| mutate | `db.exec({statement, params})` → `{affected}` |
| atomic batch | `db.tx({statements:[{statement, params}]})` |

Writes are role-gated (manager/operator); destructive `fs.remove` needs commit. Pattern — a process step writes a report (`fs.write`); an agent persists state (`db.exec`/`db.query`).

documents.title: canvas.* — CDL 2D/3D
documents.content:
# canvas.* — CDL 2D/3D

Author / validate Canvas Description Language. A CDL document begins with `type "name" { ... }`.

| do | call |
|---|---|
| CDL → JSON | `canvas.cdl_to_json({cdl})` |
| JSON → CDL | `canvas.json_to_cdl({json})` |
| validate first | `canvas.validate_cdl({cdl})` → `{ok, error?}` |

Pure value engine (no side effects). Always `validate_cdl` before rendering or persisting.

documents.title: kv.* — key/value store
documents.content:
# kv.* — key/value store

Small workspace-scoped JSON state (for bulk/relational data use `db.*`).
`kv.set({key, value})` · `kv.get({key})` · `kv.exists({key})` · `kv.keys({prefix?})` · `kv.remove({key})`.

Pattern — a skill stashes a cursor / checkpoint between runs.

documents.title: analysis.* — spec-driven analysis
documents.content:
# analysis.* — spec-driven analysis

`analysis.list_specs({search?})` → pick a spec id → `analysis.run({specId, params})` → `{jobId}` → `analysis.get_job({jobId})` for status/result.

Long jobs are async — poll `get_job`.

documents.title: channel.* — messaging
documents.content:
# channel.* — messaging

`channel.send({channelId, conversationId, text})` — `channelId:'in_app'` = internal feed (reversible, auto-allowed); external connectors gate as destructive. `channel.receive` injects an inbound message (agentic loop). `channel.list` · `channel.status` · `channel.session.history` for state.

Pattern — a process handoff posts to an in-app thread (a step's `channelThreadId`); notify a person via `channel_notify`.

documents.title: ingest.* — document chunking → facts
documents.content:
# ingest.* — document chunking → facts

`ingest.run({...})` chunks a document into retrievable pieces that feed the knowledge fact graph. In Ops, `knowledge_ingest_file` wraps this capability.

documents.title: form.* — document generation
documents.content:
# form.* — document generation

`form.list_templates` → `form.create_document({templateId, data})` → `form.render` → `form.export`. Validate with `form.validate`. Template-driven reports / documents (5 renderers).

sources.title: Studio host concepts
sources.description: Architectural concepts external LLM should know.

documents.title: Host vs Domain — superset / subset
documents.content: Studio = AppPlayer superset. AppPlayer base = renderer + atom + MCP dispatch + standard wiring. Studio adds: studio.builder.* mutators + chrome lifecycle slot fire + integrated builder UI surface (3-way switch) + studio Vbu* atoms (chrome/inspector). Bundles declare capability via manifest.requires.builtinAtoms / builtinTools — Studio fills more than AppPlayer.

documents.title: Tab vs Install — workspace project vs installed bundle
documents.content: App Builder's currentProject (under workspace/) is NOT an installed bundle — it sits in the App Builder built-in tab as the project being authored. Once packed (vibe_build_pack_bundle → .mcpb) and installed (studio.bundle.install), it gains a SEPARATE tab and appears in Home's INSTALLED APPS section. Tabs.json persists open tabs across sessions including installed ones.

documents.title: Domain MCP server inheritance — system vs per-domain
documents.content: Each domain has inheritFromSystem (default true). ON = share the system MCP server at default URL. OFF = DomainServerManager.spawn allocates a separate URL/port for narrow connection. Per MOD-INFRA-010, the per-domain server mirrors host studio.* tools + that domain's tools + knowledge. Reduces noise for external clients that work with one domain at a time.

documents.title: Tool name aliases — flat vs categorized
documents.content: App Builder's inner vibe_* tools are exposed on host with BOTH names: original vibe_<family>_<verb> AND categorized alias app_builder.<family>.<verb>. Same handler. Use either — categorized version is easier to scan in tools/list.

documents.title: Knowledge separation — host vs domain
documents.content: Studio host (studio.* tool catalog · architecture concepts) lives in seed studio_docs.mbd's knowledge.sources. Each domain's tool catalog lives in that domain's seed bundle (app_builder.mbd, scene_builder.mbd). resources/list shows the union. studio.knowledge.query searches across all. Cross-domain answers blend host + multiple domain docs.

sources.name: Built-in apps catalog
sources.description: The three built-in apps the host ships — id / label / purpose / tool surface / default agent. Manager reads this to route bundle requests.

documents.title: app_builder · App Builder
documents.content:
Built-in id: app_builder
Label: App Builder
Default chat agent: app_builder.manager (declared in seed manifest by `role: manager`).
Purpose: author mcp_ui_dsl bundles. The user describes an app in chat; the manager dispatches to specialists (`ux_designer` → wireframes · `tool_designer` → tool catalogs · `knowledge_designer` → knowledge sources · `ui_implementer` → mcp_ui_dsl JSON via `studio.builder.*` mutators · `dart_converter` / `embed_converter` → export to .app or LVGL/Qt · `debug_analyst` → diagnose).
Tool surface (high level): `studio.project.*` (new/open/save) · `studio.builder.*` (44 mutators) · `studio.knowledge.query` · `studio.chrome.reload_tab` · `studio.renderer.screenshot` · `newAppProject` · `convertToDart` · `convertToEmbed`.
Knowledge sources (consult before authoring): `app_builder_six_layers` (8 docs) · `app_builder_wiring` · `app_builder_pitfalls` · `dart_conversion_patterns` · `embed_conversion_patterns` · `mcp_ui_dsl_widgets` · `mcp_ui_dsl_state_actions` · `mcp_ui_dsl_custom_widgets` · `bundle_structure` · `chat_surface` · `ux_patterns` · `ops_patterns` · `knowledge_design_patterns` · `common_pitfalls` · `external_llm_app_builder`.
Trigger from Home: user says 'new app' / 'edit bundle' / 'export to dart' → host manager invokes `bk.agent.dispatch` with target `app_builder.manager` after ensuring the App Builder tab is open (`studio.chrome.*`).

documents.title: scene_builder · Scene Builder
documents.content:
Built-in id: scene_builder
Label: Scene Builder
Default chat agent: scene_builder.manager.
Purpose: author tutorial / demo / scenario videos as scripted MCP-tool sequences. The user describes a click-through; the manager records each step with overlay + caption + screen capture and emits an MP4.
Tool surface: `studio.scenario.*` (5 — list / save / read / run / preview) · `studio.recorder.*` (7 — start / stop / status / caption / mark / encode / recordings.list) · `studio.overlay.*` (4 — push / list / remove / clear) · `studio.scene.project.*` (3 — new / open / info) · `studio.ui.*` (tap / drag / hover) · `studio.renderer.*` (activate / current_view / layout_snapshot / screenshot).
Knowledge: `scene_builder_tool_surface` (5 docs).
Trigger from Home: user says 'record a demo' / 'capture this flow' / 'make a tutorial video' → host manager opens the Scene Builder tab and dispatches to `scene_builder.manager`.

documents.title: makemind_ops · Ops
documents.content:
Built-in id: makemind_ops
Label: Ops
Default chat agent: ops.admin.
Purpose: workspace operations — members / tasks / processes / philosophies / skills / agents / knowledge / bundles. A project-bound surface (per-project root); each project lives at `<workspaceDir>/<project>/` with marker `project.opsproj` + per-workspace `.mbd` directories.
Tool surface: `makemind_ops.*` family — workspace_create / workspace_list / member_* / task_* / process_* / skill_* / profile_* / philosophy_* / knowledge_* / bundle_* + 9 browser primitives (page_view / web_search / extract / crawl / monitor / submit_form / download / page_audit_role / page_compare_actors). Plus the kernel-standard `bk.*` surface.
Knowledge: `ops_overview` (5 docs — overview · capabilities · workspace_structure · tool_surface · agents).
Trigger from Home: user says 'manage workspace' / 'add member' / 'kick off process' → host manager opens the Ops tab and dispatches to `ops.admin`.

sources.name: Host manager routing manual
sources.description: User-intent → host action playbook. The manager consults this when deciding whether to answer inline, dispatch to a built-in agent, or call a system tool.

documents.title: Intent → route
documents.content:
INSTALL / LIST / ACTIVATE BUNDLES
  user: 'what bundles do I have' / 'install <path>' / 'open <bundle>'
  action: call `studio.bundle.list` (list) · `studio.bundle.install` (install · internal — see gate) · `studio.bundle.activate` (open new tab) directly. Report the result inline.

OPEN A BUILT-IN APP
  user: 'open App Builder' / 'launch Scene Builder' / 'go to Ops'
  action: `studio.chrome.open_seed` with the built-in id (`app_builder` / `scene_builder` / `makemind_ops`). Then dispatch to that builtin's manager via `bk.agent.dispatch` if the user's request needs in-domain work.

AUTHORING / EDITING A BUNDLE
  user: 'create a counter app' / 'add a settings page'
  action: ensure App Builder tab is open, then `bk.agent.dispatch app_builder.manager` with the user's request as the input. The App Builder manager fans out to ux_designer / ui_implementer / etc.

RECORD A DEMO / TUTORIAL VIDEO
  user: 'record a demo of …'
  action: open Scene Builder, dispatch to `scene_builder.manager`.

WORKSPACE OPERATIONS (members / tasks / processes / knowledge)
  user: 'add a member' / 'create a task' / 'list our skills'
  action: open Ops, dispatch to `ops.admin`.

INTROSPECTION / DEBUG
  user: 'what agents do we have' / 'which tools' / 'debug state'
  action: call `bk.agent.list` · `studio.meta.list_tools` · `studio.debug.config` · `studio.debug.tabs` · `studio.debug.servers` directly and report.

KNOWLEDGE QUERIES
  user: 'how do I …' / 'what does X mean'
  action: `bk.knowledge.query` first (search the seed corpus), then synthesise. Don't guess when the answer requires schema or naming detail — surface the source you cited.

Always prefer calling a tool to inventing an answer; the catalog is rich and `bk.knowledge.query` covers nearly every concept question.

documents.title: Safety rules
documents.content:
1. Never invent agent ids / tool names / bundle paths — call `bk.agent.list` / `studio.meta.list_tools` / `studio.bundle.list` first when uncertain.
2. `studio.chrome.create_package` · `studio.bundle.install` · `studio.bundle.activate` · `studio.bundle.uninstall` are internal — the host gates external callers. Surface the gate's reject message verbatim when you hit one.
3. When dispatching with `bk.agent.dispatch`, pass the user's original intent (or a clear paraphrase) as the `input`; do not over-summarise.
4. Tool errors surface as `[error] …` in this chat — relay them to the user with the corrective action (e.g. 'set the API key in Settings → LLM') instead of pretending the call succeeded.
5. When `bk.knowledge.query` returns no hits, say so and ask the user to clarify or point at a doc — don't extrapolate.

sources.name: Spec excerpts (manifest · mcp_ui_dsl · bundle)
sources.description: High-level excerpts of the schemas that govern bundles, UI, and host wiring. Detailed source-of-truth lives in `specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/` and `specs/mcp_ui_dsl/spec/1.3/`.

documents.title: mcp_bundle manifest — top-level layout
documents.content:
manifest.json top-level keys (canonical shape, mcp_bundle ≥ 0.3):
  schemaVersion · manifest{id,name,version,author,description,…} · requires{builtinAtoms[]} ·
  knowledge{sources[{id,name,description,documents[{id,title,content}]}]} ·
  agents{agents[{id,name,role,systemPrompt,model{provider,model},tools[]}]} ·
  facts{facts[]} · skills{skills[]} · profiles{profiles[]} · philosophies{philosophies[]} ·
  workflows{workflows[]} · pipelines{pipelines[]} · runbooks{runbooks[]} ·
  tools{tools[{name,description,inputSchema,kind:'js',target{entry,fn}}]} ·
  wiring{lifecycle[{slot,tool}],domainActions[{type,…}],settings[],slashCommands[],titlebar?,statusbar?} ·
  settings{sections[{label,fields[{key,label,type,…}]}]} ·
  chat{slashCommands[]} ·
  ui (mcp_ui_dsl entry path — usually `ui/app.json`)

Built-in seeds keep this shape minimal — typically `manifest + requires + schemaVersion + agents + knowledge` only. The code mounts UI / tools / wiring / settings / chat directly, so those five categories stay empty (seed-cleanliness rule, DDD apps.md §0.2).

documents.title: agents block — manager role + tools
documents.content:
agents{agents[{id, name, role, systemPrompt, model:{provider,model}, tools:[…]}]}

  · id        — local id within the bundle (`manager`, `ux_designer`, …). The host prefixes with the bundle's exposedShortId at registration (e.g. `app_builder.manager`). The HOST seed (studio.mbd) is the one exception — its agents register with the raw id (no prefix).
  · role      — `manager` / `worker` / `reviewer`. The chat panel routes to the `role: manager` entry by default (studio convention).
  · model     — { provider: 'anthropic'|'openai'|'gemini'|'claude_code', model: <catalog id> }. Settings → Domain → Agents writes here.
  · tools     — allow-list of tool names the agent may call. EMPTY list = wildcard (all system tools available). The host manager uses the wildcard.
  · systemPrompt — verbatim instruction the LLM receives every turn. Include routing rules + knowledge-source ids the agent should query.

documents.title: Tool call protocol (chat dispatch)
documents.content:
When the agent's LLM reply contains `toolCalls`, the host runs a tool-use loop:
  1. host dispatches each `(name, arguments)` through `ServerBootstrap.callTool` (same path external MCP clients use).
  2. The result is serialised as text — `[error] …` when the tool reported `isError: true`, otherwise the joined text content — and fed back as the next `ask` input.
  3. Loop continues until the model returns content with no toolCalls, or the iteration cap (8) is hit.

Implication for the manager:
  · Do issue toolCalls when you need data or to dispatch — don't paraphrase a missing answer.
  · Treat tool results as authoritative; quote them rather than rewording.
  · `bk.agent.dispatch` invokes another agent end-to-end (their own tool-use loop runs inside) and returns the result — the manager only sees the final text, not the inner turns.

sources.name: Platform 3-layer structure and tool registration paths
sources.description: The 3-layer responsibilities (UI surface / bundle interface / kernel logic), coupling points, and the three tool registration paths. Advisory reference for authoring hosts and bundles. Source: specs/platform/01-layers.md, 02-bundle-interface.md, 06-tool-registry.md, 07-knowledge-access.md.

documents.title: 3 layers — responsibilities and coupling points
documents.content:
UI layer: user surface only (chrome, runtime, widgets, DSL rendering, input, transport). Does not call kernel facades directly — goes through dispatcher or endpoint. Free to be Flutter, web, embedded, voice, etc.

Bundle layer: the manifest contract. Categories: tools.tools[], agents, skills/profiles/philosophies/facts (4 axis), flows/pipelines/runbooks, ui, wiring, settings, chat. The bundle is self-contained — any host reads the same manifest interface.

Kernel layer: headless operations logic. Owns: 8 facades (facts/skills/profiles/philosophies/workflows/pipelines/runbooks/agents), 41 standardTools (bk.<facade>.<verb>), per-agent capability scoping, BundleSessionBridge (scopeId + alias), HostToolRegistry (general tool registry), KernelServerHost/KernelClientHost abstractions, LLM pool.

Coupling: UI mounts manifest.ui and wires manifest.wiring slots. Bundle activation connects each manifest category to the kernel (BundleActivation.activate). Cross-bundle communication is indirect: fact_graph mediated, or explicit bk.<bundleId>.<rest> alias calls.

Reference: specs/platform/01-layers.md

documents.title: Tool registration — 3 paths and when to use each
documents.content:
Three registration paths determine how a tool is exposed:

1. addStandardTools(app): host home context. Registers the 41 bk.* standard tools at the endpoint with canonical names (bk.<facade>.<verb>). Intended for the host's own in-process use (master session). Schema is a permissive placeholder — not for external strict dispatch.

2. BundleSessionBridge.registerTool: knowledge tool domain wrapping. Name MUST start with 'bk.' (enforced by validator — ArgumentError otherwise). Registers in the bridge's dispatch layer AND automatically publishes bk.<bundleId>.<rest> alias on the external endpoint for each active non-master session. scopeId applied automatically. Use this when a bundle domain needs the standard 41 knowledge tools with isolation.

3. HostToolRegistry.registerExposed: general tools. Covers host-side logic tools and bundle manifest.tools.tools[] entries. Automatically prefixes exposedName as <bundleId>.<rawName>. Registers on both dispatcher and endpoint simultaneously. Two bundles may declare the same raw name without conflict.

Selection rule: host home bk.* in-process → path 1. Bundle knowledge tool with external alias → path 2. Domain logic tool or manifest tool → path 3.

Reference: specs/platform/06-tool-registry.md

documents.title: scopeId isolation — bundle namespace in knowledge access
documents.content:
BundleSessionBridge applies scopeId automatically for non-master sessions. When a bundle script calls bk.fact.write({id: 'alice', ...}), the bridge prepends the bundleId: actual storage key becomes '<bundleId>.alice'. The script never sees the prefix — it uses bare ids within its own namespace.

External callers use the alias bk.<bundleId>.fact.query — routed through the same bridge session, same scopeId context.

Cross-bundle access requires explicit prefix: kb://fact/other_bundle.alice or bk.other_bundle.fact.query. Bridge does not auto-prefix for explicit cross-bundle calls.

master session (host home): scopeId is pass-through — ids stored as-is.

Reference: specs/platform/07-knowledge-access.md

sources.name: Framework composition patterns — 5 standard patterns
sources.description: The 5 standard host composition patterns. Same framework layers + same manifest spec + different wiring = different products. Advisory for choosing the right pattern when authoring a host or domain. Source: specs/platform/05-composition-patterns.md.

documents.title: 5 composition patterns — overview and selection
documents.content:
Composition varies across 5 dimensions: bundle source (local .mbd / remote MCP server / both), UI source (host chrome / manifest.ui / remote), tool source (host / manifest / external server / kernel standard), transport (in-process / server / client / both), bundle count (single / multi).

Pattern 1 — server-serve (thin client): server hosts KernelApp + bundle, serves UI resources and tools over MCP. Client is a thin player (renderer + tool-call forwarder). Example: AppPlayer connecting to an external MCP server.

Pattern 2 — client-only (local self-contained): single process, local .mbd, no external server. UI and tools all in-process. serverHostFactory null. Example: AppPlayer Standard default mode.

Pattern 3 — hybrid (local + remote): local bundle active, host runs its own server endpoint AND connects to external MCP servers for additional tools/knowledge. Example: vibe_studio — local bundles + self endpoint + external LLM MCP.

Pattern 4 — multi-bundle (cross-domain, one host): multiple bundle activations in one host. Each gets its own BundleSessionBridge session and scopeId. Cross-bundle communication via fact_graph mediation, not direct. Example: vibe_studio multi-tab.

Pattern 5 — multi-server (federation): host orchestrator connects to multiple external MCP servers (per department/domain), combines tool catalogs, LLM agent decides cross-server calls. Example: enterprise environment with per-department MCP servers.

A single host may combine multiple patterns simultaneously (e.g., Pattern 3 + Pattern 4). Pattern changes require only host boot wiring changes — kernel and bundle spec unchanged.

Reference: specs/platform/05-composition-patterns.md

documents.title: Pattern selection — tradeoffs and cases
documents.content:
When building a new product instance, choosing a pattern:

· Single user, local-only, no network: Pattern 2 (client-only). Simplest boot — serverHostFactory null, in-process dispatcher.
· App that needs to receive LLM/external tool calls: Pattern 3 (hybrid). Add server endpoint alongside local bundle.
· Multi-domain workspace (several independent domains active): Pattern 4 (multi-bundle). Each domain's bundle activates with its own BundleSessionBridge session; UI tabs map to sessions.
· Thin client that plays content from a remote provider: Pattern 1 (server-serve). Client only needs renderer + MCP client connection.
· Enterprise cross-system orchestration: Pattern 5 (multi-server). Host connects to N servers, agent catalog = union.

Same manifest spec, any pattern — the bundle author does not need to know which pattern the host uses.

Reference: specs/platform/05-composition-patterns.md

sources.name: Bundle authoring runbook — three app kinds and authoring flow
sources.description: Procedural guide for authoring manifests: the three app kinds, manifest category checklist, and wiring steps. Advisory tone — cases and procedure, not hard rules. Source: specs/platform/02-bundle-interface.md, 04-ui-host.md.

documents.title: Three app kinds — studio app, server app, bundle app
documents.content:
Studio app (built-in): registered as a BuiltInApp in host code. UI + tools + wiring all wired in code at boot. Seed manifests (studio.mbd, app_builder.mbd, etc.) declare only agents + knowledge — code handles the rest. Suitable for host-level capabilities that ship with the binary.

Server app: a host that runs a KernelApp + bundle and exposes it over MCP (Pattern 1 or Pattern 3). The server serves manifest.json at bundle://manifest.json, UI at ui://, tools at tools/list, knowledge at kb://. Clients reconstruct and run the bundle identically to a local install. Suitable for multi-tenant or remotely-distributed bundles.

Bundle app (.mbd / .mcpb): a self-contained .mbd directory or packed .mcpb archive installed into a host (studio.bundle.install). The host activates it via BundleActivation. Suitable for user-authored apps, marketplace distribution, domain extensions.

Choosing: if the capability is host-core (ships in binary, always on) → studio app (built-in). If the app must be distributed to remote clients as a running service → server app. For everything else — user apps, marketplace, domain knowledge bundles → bundle app.

Reference: specs/platform/00-overview.md, 04-ui-host.md

documents.title: Manifest authoring — category checklist and procedure
documents.content:
Manifest categories and their authoring steps:

1. Identity (manifest.id/name/version/description): set first. id must be unique across the host's bundle pool.

2. requires.builtinAtoms[]: list the host capability categories the bundle's JS / DSL surface calls (e.g. 'mcp', 'fs', 'ui', 'workspace', 'kb', 'agent', 'bus', 'bundle'). These are coarse host-bridge atoms (host.<atom>.<verb>), NOT mcp_ui_dsl widget atoms. Strict gate — host refuses activation when an entry is not in its advertised set. Leave empty for a fully portable bundle.

3. tools.tools[]: domain logic tools. Each entry needs name (unique within bundle), description, inputSchema, kind ('js' for in-bundle JS), target.entry (path in tools/) and target.fn. Name must NOT start with 'bk.' — those are reserved for knowledge tools. Host will expose as <bundleId>.<rawName>.

4. agents[]: one entry per agent. Assign id, name, role (manager/worker/reviewer), model, systemPrompt. Add tools allowlist only when narrowing from role default is needed (see studio_agent_scoping source). The manager agent's systemPrompt should include routing rules and which knowledge sources to query.

5. 4 axis (skills/profiles/philosophies/facts): declare domain knowledge assets. BundleActivation registers them automatically. ids are scoped to the bundle namespace at activation.

6. ui: path to the entry mcp_ui_dsl JSON (e.g. 'ui/app.json'). Omit if the bundle has no UI.

7. wiring: connect chrome slots. lifecycle[] maps standard slot ids (project.new/open/save etc.) to tool names. domainActions[] declares row-2 icon buttons. titlebar/statusbar set text templates. settings[] lists settings entries.

8. settings.sections: declare inline settings fields (type: secret/string/bool/select etc.). Host settings dialog renders these; changes autosave.

9. knowledge.sources: advisory reference documents for LLM agents. Each source has id, name, description, documents[{id, title, content}].

For seed bundles (built-ins): only categories 4 and 9 are typically needed — tools/ui/wiring/settings are wired in code.

Reference: specs/platform/02-bundle-interface.md

documents.title: Wiring authoring — chrome slot connection patterns
documents.content:
wiring.lifecycle connects standard chrome button slots to bundle tool calls:
  { "slot": "project.new", "tool": "myCreateTool" }
Standard slots include: project.new, project.open, project.save, project.close, project.export, edit.undo, edit.redo, history.show, edit.revert. Only declare slots the bundle actually handles.

wiring.domainActions declares row-2 domain icon buttons in chrome. Each entry needs tool (raw name within bundle — host prefixes <bundleId>. automatically), icon, and tooltip.

wiring.titlebar / wiring.statusbar: text with optional state binding {{key}} templates. Key values come from the bundle's runtime state.

wiring.settings: entries shown in the domain settings panel, separate from settings.sections fields.

chat.slashCommands: slash chips in the chat composer. { command: '/refresh', tool: 'refresh' } — host auto-prefixes the tool name with bundleId on invocation.

Reference: specs/platform/02-bundle-interface.md §6

sources.name: MCP serving — bundle as MCP server
sources.description: How a bundle is served over MCP so a client runs it identically to a local install. Entry point, address map, equivalence guarantee, and partial-server compatibility. Source: specs/mcp_serving/spec/1.0/README.md.

documents.title: MCP serving — entry point and address map
documents.content:
A bundle served over MCP uses these canonical addresses:

  bundle://manifest.json — the bundle document (top-level manifest.json). Reading this yields: manifest metadata, requires, settings, wiring, chat, tool declarations, ui config, and declared knowledge section names. This is the entry point for a fully-described bundle.

  ui://app — the UI application definition (mcp_ui_dsl). ui://page/{name} for individual pages.

  kb://<facade>/<id> — knowledge entries (facts, skills, profiles, etc.).

  tools/list + tools/call — executable tools.

  bundle://<path> — assets (icons, fonts, images).

The client reads bundle://manifest.json first, then fetches each declared payload at its own address. The result is reconstructed into an in-memory Bundle and run through the same activation path a locally-installed bundle uses.

Reference: specs/mcp_serving/spec/1.0/README.md

documents.title: Equivalence guarantee and partial server compatibility
documents.content:
Equivalence: a served bundle MUST behave identically to running the same bundle locally (local path: install → kernel boot → knowledge + standard tools registered → settings store → UI built). Serving changes the source, not the execution.

Partial server: any subset of the above addresses MAY be served. A client MUST ignore unknown parts without error. A server that predates mcp_serving 1.0 (a pure ui://app mcp_ui_dsl server, a tools-only server) is a valid partial bundle and runs unchanged — the client falls back to baseline mcp_ui_dsl init flow.

Source-agnostic: the server MAY serve from a .mbd on disk or generate content in code. The client depends only on the addresses, never on the server's source.

When authoring a server app: expose bundle://manifest.json if clients need full bundle metadata (settings, wiring, agents). Omit it if clients only need UI + tools (existing ui://app flow is sufficient).

Reference: specs/mcp_serving/spec/1.0/README.md

sources.name: Per-agent capability scoping
sources.description: How agent tool and knowledge access is scoped by role and manifest allowlist. The core value of having a kernel. Advisory for manifest authoring and agent design. Source: specs/platform/10-agent-scoping.md.

documents.title: Why scoping matters — kernel value
documents.content:
Without per-agent scoping, every agent sees the full tool catalog and full knowledge asset set — equivalent to one monolithic LLM handling everything. The value of multi-agent only materializes when each agent receives only the subset it needs.

Scoping provides: specialization (researcher gets knowledge tools only / coder gets edit tools only / manager gets delegation tools only), token efficiency (each call builds prompt from own assets only, not full catalog), LLM accuracy (5 tools vs 100 tools — correct selection rate goes up), context isolation (one agent's assets don't noise another agent's decisions), security (worker can't call host.* tools), multi-agent orchestration (manager forced to delegate because it only has delegation tools).

Reference: specs/platform/10-agent-scoping.md

documents.title: Role defaults — manager, worker, reviewer
documents.content:
Three roles with framework-default tool subsets:

manager: bk.agent.* delegation tools + bk.*.query/get/list read tools + workflow/pipeline/runbook status. Mutation tools excluded. Host-specific namespaces (studio.*, app.*, arbitrary) also excluded from default — kernel does not hard-code any host's prefix. To give the manager host tools, add them to manifest agents[i].tools allowlist explicitly.

worker: own bundle's non-master session subset. bk.<bundleId>.* alias tools + <bundleId>.* domain tools. Non-master session means scopeId isolation automatically applied.

reviewer: master session read subset (knowledge read + bk.agent.history). No delegation tools.

manager uses master session; worker and reviewer use non-master (bundle-scoped).

For a specialist agent narrower than worker default: declare tools allowlist explicitly in the manifest. No new role enum needed.

Reference: specs/platform/10-agent-scoping.md

documents.title: Manifest allowlist and 4-axis domain scope
documents.content:
Manifest agents[i].tools allowlist: glob patterns that override the role default. Examples:
  ["bk.fact.*", "bk.skill.execute", "<bundleId>.editor.open"]
  ["bk.agent.list", "bk.agent.dispatch", "studio.*"]

Empty / omitted tools: role default applies (not wildcard-all). Explicit empty list is not the same as omitted — check host implementation.

4 axis (skills / profiles / philosophies / facts) and knowledge resources (kb://) are also scoped by BundleActivation scopeId and session type. This is separate from the tools allowlist: 4-axis controls what knowledge assets an agent's LLM prompt is composed from, while the tools allowlist controls which executable tools the agent may call. A domain worker automatically receives only its own bundle's 4-axis assets in prompt synthesis — no allowlist needed for this dimension.

Anti-patterns to avoid: giving manager the full catalog (monolithic LLM), giving all agents master session (defeats isolation), creating fallback catalogs when tools not declared (throw instead, so misconfiguration is visible).

Reference: specs/platform/10-agent-scoping.md

sources.name: Bundle manifest schema — authoring reference
sources.description: Key shapes, sections, ToolKind, folder layout, and authoring cases for mcp_bundle 1.0. Advisory reference — not a schema dump. Source: specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/.

documents.title: Top-level keys — what lives where
documents.content:
Every manifest.json is an McpBundle. Required: schemaVersion (always "1.0.0") + manifest.id/name/version. Everything else is optional.

Top-level section keys and what they hold:
  schemaVersion — "1.0.0" fixed for this spec version.
  manifest — identity + wiring + chrome slots (id, name, version, description, provider, wiring, chat, type, entryPoint, tags, dependencies).
  requires — portability contract: builtinAtoms[] + builtinTools[]. Strict gates — host refuses activation when unmet. Atoms: mcp, fs, ui, workspace, kb, agent, bus, bundle.
  tools — tools.tools[] each entry: {name, kind, target, description, inputSchema, outputSchema, agentScope}. name must be unique within section and non-empty.
  agents — agents.agents[]: {id, name, role, systemPrompt, model{provider,model}, tools[], profileIds, skillIds, philosophyIds, factSourceIds, behavior}.
  knowledge — knowledge.sources[]: {id, name, description, documents[{id,title,content}]} + retriever + index config.
  facts / workflows / pipelines / runbooks — 4 knowledge axis + flow types.
  skills — skill modules with procedure graphs + rubrics.
  profiles — system-prompt building blocks (ProfileDefinition.sections[]).
  philosophy — guiding principles + examples/counter-examples.
  ui — deprecated inline field; canonical UI lives in ui/ folder as ui/app.json.
  wiring — chrome slot bindings (domainActions / lifecycle / settings). Canonical position is TOP-LEVEL (sibling of manifest / tools / requires), alongside chat and settings. The manifest-nested form (manifest.wiring / manifest.chat / manifest.settings) is a LEGACY alias only — new bundles MUST emit at top-level; loaders accept both and top-level wins on collision.
  extensions — free-form host-specific data; round-trips verbatim.

Seed bundles (built-ins) typically carry only manifest + requires + schemaVersion + agents + knowledge — tools/ui/wiring/settings are registered in host code.

Reference: specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/01_Bundle_Schema.md, 02_Manifest.md

documents.title: Section + folder layout — inline vs on-disk
documents.content:
Most content sections can live EITHER inline in manifest.json OR as files in the matching reserved folder. Folder content takes precedence on id conflict.

Section → reserved folder mapping:
  ui            → ui/         (canonical; inline ui field is deprecated)
  assets        → assets/
  skills        → skills/
  knowledge     → knowledge/
  facts         → facts/
  workflows     → workflows/
  pipelines     → pipelines/
  runbooks      → runbooks/
  tools         → tools/       (also holds JS source files for kind:js tools)
  profiles      → profiles/
  philosophy    → philosophy/
  agents        → agents/

Inline-only sections (no reserved folder in v1.0): requires, bindings, tests, policies, factGraphSchema, factGraphSection, compatibility, integrity, extensions.

A minimal .mbd directory:
  mybundle.mbd/
    manifest.json       ← only required file
    ui/app.json         ← mcp_ui_dsl 1.3 ApplicationDefinition
    tools/export.js     ← JS tool implementation

The bundle format owns 12 reserved folder names (declaration order): ui / assets / skills / knowledge / facts / workflows / pipelines / runbooks / tools / profiles / philosophy / agents. Binary / text assets (icons, fonts, files) live under assets/ — there is no sources/ folder.

Reference: specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/01_Bundle_Schema.md §1.5, 07_Resources.md

documents.title: ToolKind enum — dispatch discriminator
documents.content:
tools.tools[].kind controls how the host dispatches the tool.

  host   — host builtin (target: {}). The host already knows the dispatch path. Domain bundles rarely declare these — the host registers them itself. Use when documenting a wiring slot reference or for agentScope narrowing.
  js     — bundled JavaScript in flutter_js (target: {entry: "tools/foo.js", fn: "exportAll"}). Most domain tools use this. Each bundle gets an isolated JS context. The global `host.<atom>.<verb>(args)` bridge provides access to mcp.callTool, fs, ui, kb, agent, workspace, bundle, bus.
  mcp    — external MCP server (target: {transport: "http"|"stdio", url?, command?, args?}). For outbound connections to remote services.
  cloud  — HTTPS endpoint (target: {url: "https://..."}). Thin wrapper over a POST call.
  unknown — forward-compat round-trip slot; validator rejects this as input.

Naming: tool name MUST be non-empty and unique within the section. Conventionally namespaced as <bundle>.<verb>. Names starting with 'bk.' are reserved for knowledge bridge tools — domain tools must not use that prefix.

The host exposes tools as <bundleId>.<rawName> on the MCP endpoint (via HostToolRegistry.registerExposed). JS tools access all MCP tools (own + host + other bundles) through host.mcp.callTool(name, args).

Reference: specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/04_Tools.md

documents.title: Authoring cases — when to use which field
documents.content:
Common authoring decisions and which fields they map to:

New UI-bearing app: set manifest.id/name/version + create ui/app.json (mcp_ui_dsl ApplicationDefinition). Add requires.builtinAtoms: ["mcp", "ui"] at minimum. Wiring slots (project.save etc.) go in manifest.wiring.lifecycle.

Domain logic tools: add tools.tools[] entries with kind:js. JS file goes in tools/<name>.js. Declare the atoms the JS needs in requires.builtinAtoms. Use host.mcp.callTool for calling back into host or other bundles.

LLM agents: add agents.agents[] with id, role (manager/worker/reviewer), model, systemPrompt, tools allowlist. The manager's tools array should include delegation tools (bk.agent.*) + any domain tools the manager coordinates. Workers get their own domain tool subset.

Knowledge / advisory docs for LLM agents: add knowledge.sources[] with id, name, documents[]. Each document: id, title, content (plain text or markdown). Advisory tone — not hardcoded rules.

Chrome slot wiring: manifest.wiring.lifecycle maps standard slot ids (project.new / project.open / project.save / project.close / project.export / edit.undo / edit.redo) to tool names declared in tools[]. manifest.wiring.domainActions declares row-2 icon buttons: {tool, icon, tooltip}.

Settings schema: manifest.settings.sections[].fields[]. Types include secret / string / bool / select. Host renders and autosaves. Also appears in manifest.wiring.settings for domain settings panel entries.

Seed bundle (built-in): agents + knowledge only in manifest.json. Tools/UI/wiring registered in host Dart code. Keeps manifest minimal.

For additive patches: add new sections/entries without changing schemaVersion — the spec treats sections as independent and additive patches are safe.

Reference: specs/mcp_bundle/spec/1.0/02_Manifest.md, 03_Sections.md, 04_Tools.md, 06_Wiring.md

sources.name: mcp_ui_dsl 1.3 — authoring reference
sources.description: Widget catalog overview, canonical shapes, common mistakes, and layout basics for mcp_ui_dsl 1.3. Property ground truth is widgets/<cat>/<name>.yaml — prose md may lag. Source: specs/mcp_ui_dsl/spec/1.3/.

documents.title: Definition tree — Application / Page / Widget
documents.content:
Every mcp_ui_dsl program is one of three shapes:

  ApplicationDefinition (type: "application") — top-level container. Required fields: type, routes (map of path → PageDefinition or "ui://" URI). App-scope state: state.initial.* (accessible as {{app.*}}). Navigation chrome: navigation.type (drawer/bottomBar/rail/tabs) + navigation.items.

  PageDefinition (type: "page") — full-screen view. Required: type, content (root widget). Page-scope state: state.initial.* (accessible as {{page.*}} or bare identifier). Legacy alias: "screen" is accepted but emitters must use "page".

  WidgetDefinition — any widget node. Required: type (the widget's canonical type name). Children via child (single) or children (array).

Common shapes:
  state.initial block: {"initial": {"count": 0, "items": []}}
  Binding: {{count}} / {{page.count}} / {{app.user.name}} / {{count > 0 ? "yes" : "no"}}
  Frequently wrong: writing state: {"count": 0} instead of state: {"initial": {"count": 0}}.

Entry file: ui/app.json inside the .mbd directory. The manifest.json does not inline UI — it lives in the folder.

Reference: specs/mcp_ui_dsl/spec/1.3/01_Core_Concepts.md

documents.title: Widget catalog — categories and representative widgets
documents.content:
Widget categories in the Core Profile (property ground truth = widgets/<cat>/<name>.yaml):

  layout: box, linear, stack, center, align, padding, margin, expanded, flexible, spacer, wrap, positioned, safeArea, sizedBox, aspectRatio, conditional, visibility, indexedStack.
  display: text, richText, image, icon, card, divider, verticalDivider, badge, chip, avatar, tooltip, placeholder, progressBar, banner, decoration.
  input: button, iconButton, textInput, toggle, select, checkbox, checkboxGroup, radio, radioGroup, slider, rangeSlider, numberField, form, rating, dateField, timeField, datePicker, timePicker, dateRangePicker, colorPicker, segmentedControl, stepper, numberStepper.
  list: list, grid, listItem.
  navigation: headerBar, bottomNavigation, tabBar, tabBarView, drawer, navigationRail, floatingActionButton, popupMenuButton.
  scroll: scrollView, singleChildScrollView, scrollBar, pageView.
  interaction: gestureDetector, inkWell, draggable, dragTarget.
  dialog: alertDialog, simpleDialog, customDialog, snackBar, bottomSheet.
  animation: animatedContainer, opacity (v1.3), transform (v1.3), lottieAnimation.
  utility: lazy, fittedBox, clipOval, clipRRect.
  template: use, template (see 09_Templates.md).

Advanced widgets (chart, canvas, codeEditor, terminal, etc.) are defined in 10_Advanced_Widgets.md — they require the Advanced Profile.

Common properties every widget accepts: type, child, children, visible, tooltip, click (universal tap wrapper), accessibility, key.

Reference: specs/mcp_ui_dsl/spec/1.3/02_Widgets.md, widgets/<cat>/<name>.yaml

documents.title: Canonical shapes — frequently wrong patterns
documents.content:
These are the shapes that LLM generators most often get wrong. Canonical form is the only valid form for emitters.

1. text widget content field
   Canonical field: text (NOT content, NOT label, NOT value).
   content is a legacy alias — accepted on input but must not be emitted.
   {"type": "text", "text": "Hello {{user.name}}"}

2. text.variant — 15 M3 typography roles (camelCase)
   displayLarge / displayMedium / displaySmall /
   headlineLarge / headlineMedium / headlineSmall /
   titleLarge / titleMedium / titleSmall /
   bodyLarge / bodyMedium / bodySmall /
   labelLarge / labelMedium / labelSmall
   Wrong: "display-large" / "Display Large" / "headline" (no size suffix).
   variant resolves through theme.typography.<variant>. style layered on top.

3. button label field
   Canonical field: label (NOT text). text is a legacy alias.
   {"type": "button", "label": "Submit", "onTap": {"type": "tool", "tool": "save"}}

4. state action shape
   Canonical: {"type": "state", "action": "set", "binding": "count", "value": 1}
   Sub-actions: set / increment / decrement / toggle / append / pop / remove / removeAt.
   Wrong: {"type": "setState"} / {"type": "state", "key": "count"} / omitting "action" field.

5. state.initial structure
   Page/app state must be wrapped: state: {"initial": {"count": 0}}
   Wrong: state: {"count": 0} — flattened initial values do not work.

6. FormFactor (responsive) — 5+1 classes
   compact (<600dp) / medium (600–839) / expanded (840–1199) / large (1200–1599) / extraLarge (≥1600) / embedded (host-pinned).
   Used in responsive property values and mediaQuery widget. Class names are camelCase.

Reference: specs/mcp_ui_dsl/spec/1.3/widgets/display/text.yaml, widgets/input/button.yaml, 04_Actions.md §4.2, 01_Core_Concepts.md §1.3, 14_Responsive_Events.md §14.1

documents.title: Layout basics and action types
